summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the secret to blogging success is deceptively simple: pick a posting schedule and stick to it consistently for years. He shares his own journey from writing in obscurity for a year to reaching 100,000 daily readers, attributing his success not to talent but to persistent effort and incremental improvement. The post draws parallels between blogging consistency and the 'always be jabbing' philosophy in boxing—continuous small efforts combined with occasional bigger pieces. Atwood emphasizes that through years of consistent writing, he gained career opportunities, industry connections, and recognition that he never anticipated, making blogging the most important thing he's done for his career.
key_points: Consistent posting schedule matters more than writing quality, audience size, or having interesting things to say—commitment to showing up is the foundation
Success requires patience measured in years, not months, which weeds out most people who give up too early
Each post provides compound benefits: improved writing skills, deeper topic knowledge, community connections, and feedback loops
Blogging success opened unexpected career doors including job opportunities, conference invitations, book deals, and relationships with industry icons
The approach requires no special talent or strategy—just incremental improvement and a 'suck less every year' mentality
money_quotes: Pick a schedule you can live with, and stick to it. Until you do that, none of the other advice I could give you will matter.
Every time I wrote, I tried to write something just the tiniest bit better than I did last time.

The changes, to me, were almost imperceptible. But from a very modest start – a 2004 new year's resolution for professional development – I'd say writing this blog is now, without a doubt, the most important thing I've ever done in my entire career.
I achieved all this without being particularly talented.
It was done one small post at a time, with no real planning or strategy whatsoever, beyond the simple incremental suck less every year kind.
If I can achieve this kind of success with my blog, so can you.
I wrote this blog for a year in utter obscurity, but I kept at it because I enjoyed it.
key_insight: Blogging success comes not from talent or strategy, but from the unglamorous discipline of showing up consistently over years while focusing on incremental improvement.
